talk_title: Mechanistic insights into KRAS activation and novel direct KRAS inhibitors

speaker.name: Frank McCormick
speaker.affiliation: Frederick National Laboratory for Cancer Research

MOA_summary: McCormick described a covalent inhibitor that targets a histidine-95 residue unique to KRAS, allowing binding to the GTP‑bound state and shifting the switch‑one region to an inactive state‑one conformation. He also presented a PI3K‑alpha “breaker” that blocks the Ras‑PI3K interaction, increasing affinity from ~20 µM to ~6 nM and preventing downstream signaling.
key_findings: SHP2 phosphatase activity is dispensable for RAS activation, indicating a non‑catalytic role in SOS‑mediated signaling.
A covalent inhibitor targeting KRAS histidine‑95 can engage both GDP‑ and GTP‑bound KRAS, overcoming the limitation of off‑state inhibitors.
Binding of the inhibitor to the switch‑two pocket forces switch‑one into an unproductive state‑one conformation, blocking effector interactions.
The PI3K‑alpha breaker increases KRAS‑PI3K binding affinity to low‑nanomolar range, enabling selective disruption of KRAS‑driven PI3K signaling.
Structural studies reveal distinct conformations of RAF activation complexes that may be exploitable for next‑generation RAF inhibitors.

talk_title: Clinical activity of the KRAS G12D inhibitor 3034 combined with EGFR blockade

biomarkers.name: KRAS G12D ctDNA
biomarkers.finding: ≥90% reduction in ctDNA levels observed in majority of patients across tumor types

biomarkers.name: CA19-9
biomarkers.finding: >90% decline in CA19-9 concurrent with radiographic response in pancreatic cancer patient

models.name: QTX-3034 study
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12D mutant colorectal, pancreatic, endometrial cancers; some with MTAP loss

clinical_data.population: KRAS G12D mutant across CRC, PDAC, endometrial

clinical_data.value: partial responses observed; durable responses noted
comparable between monotherapy and combination over 6 months
majority of mice showed regression; in patient, deep partial response sustained >9 months
clinical_data.n: preclinical mouse n=??; clinical patient case n=1
clinical_data.population: HPAC pancreatic xenograft; patient with KRAS G12D PDAC

clinical_data.value: 300 days until radiographic PD after combination therapy
clinical_data.population: KRAS G12D PDAC with MTAP loss

MOA_summary: Pitt presented the allosteric KRAS inhibitor 3034, which binds the switch‑two pocket and forces switch‑one into an inactive conformation, thereby blocking effector signaling of both G12D and G12V mutants. When combined with EGFR inhibition (cetuximab), the regimen suppresses wild‑type KRAS amplification and upstream RTK feedback, leading to deep tumor regressions and >90% ctDNA clearance in KRAS‑mutant colorectal, pancreatic, and endometrial cancers.
readouts: tumor volume measurements in xenograft models
key_findings: 3034 monotherapy slowed tumor growth in KRAS G12D HPAC xenografts but did not induce regression.
Combination of 3034 with cetuximab produced tumor regressions in the majority of mice and deep partial responses in patients.
≥90% reduction in KRAS G12D ctDNA correlated with radiographic responses across colorectal, pancreatic, and endometrial cohorts.
No increase in cetuximab‑related rash or Grade ≥3 adverse events observed with the combination.
Acquired resistance manifested as KRAS wild‑type amplification after ~300 days of therapy, highlighting a limitation of the current inhibitor’s coverage of wild‑type KRAS.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Acquired KRAS wild‑type amplification led to disease progression despite continued KRAS G12D inhibition.

combination_strategies.rationale: EGFR blockade drives KRAS into inactive state, enhancing allosteric inhibitor engagement and preventing feedback activation.
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic tumor regression in preclinical models and objective responses in patients

combination_strategies.rationale: Early‑line combination may increase depth of response in KRAS‑mutant colorectal and pancreatic cancers.
combination_strategies.phenotype: planned evaluation in first‑ and second‑line settings

combination_strategies.rationale: Broad RTK blockade to further suppress upstream signaling and enhance KRAS inhibition.
combination_strategies.phenotype: preclinical synergy across KRAS mutant models

open_questions: How can wild‑type KRAS amplification be effectively targeted alongside mutant KRAS inhibition?
What is the optimal dosing strategy to achieve EC90 coverage across all KRAS alleles while maintaining tolerability?

talk_title: Preclinical and early clinical evaluation of a pan‑KRAS on/off state inhibitor (3034)

biomarkers.name: ctDNA
biomarkers.finding: early ctDNA suppression correlated with tumor response in dose‑escalation patients

MOA_summary: The oral pan‑KRAS inhibitor 3034 binds both the inactive and active states of KRAS, suppressing downstream MAPK signaling and shifting the tumor microenvironment toward immune activation while reducing immunosuppressive signals, leading to tumor growth inhibition and shrinkage that can be enhanced by EGFR blockade.
key_findings: Endometrioid endometrial cancer patients showed robust and durable responses despite co‑occurring PI3K pathway alterations.
Combination of 3034 with cetuximab prevented outgrowth observed with monotherapy in preclinical models.
Preclinical mouse studies demonstrated tumor shrinkage at low oral doses (10‑30 mg) with reduced gastrointestinal adverse events compared with earlier KRAS inhibitors.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Monotherapy led to outgrowth over time, suggesting adaptive resistance mechanisms.
Preliminary data indicate possible KRAS allele amplification as a resistance mechanism in patients.

combination_strategies.rationale: Upstream EGFR blockade suppresses compensatory MAPK re‑activation seen with KRAS inhibition alone.
combination_strategies.phenotype: prevents tumor outgrowth and enhances shrinkage in xenograft models

combination_strategies.rationale: Concurrent inhibition of upstream SHP2 could more completely shut down RAS signaling.
combination_strategies.phenotype: potentially higher efficacy but increased toxicity observed in early studies

open_questions: What specific genotypic features (e.g., PI3K mutation status) drive the observed responses in endometrial cancer?
Does PI3K wild‑type versus mutant status affect early ctDNA suppression and durability of response?

talk_title: Clinical development of multi‑selective KRAS tricomplex inhibitors (Deruxon Rasib) and combination strategies

targets.dependency_context: KRAS mutant solid tumors (NSCLC, PDAC, CRC)

biomarkers.name: Disease Control Rate
biomarkers.finding: Overall DCR 95% in Resolute‑302 trial (n not specified)

biomarkers.name: Progression‑Free Survival
biomarkers.finding: Lower bound of 95% CI for PFS exceeded median PFS of standard second‑line chemotherapy

biomarkers.name: Overall Survival
biomarkers.finding: Lower bound of 95% CI for OS exceeded median OS of standard second‑line chemotherapy

models.name: Resolute‑302 (Phase III)
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS mutant solid tumors, includes G12, G13, Q61 variants

clinical_data.value: lower 95% CI > median PFS of standard care
lower 95% CI > median OS of standard care

MOA_summary: Deruxon Rasib forms a tricomplex with cyclophilin A and KRAS, sterically blocking effector binding and stimulating GTP hydrolysis, thereby shutting down MAPK and PI3K signaling across multiple KRAS mutants; this dual mechanism yields tumor regression and induces immune activation without significant liver toxicity.
readouts: in vivo tumor xenograft growth curves
key_findings: Resolute‑302 met its primary and secondary endpoints with a disease control rate of 95% in KRAS mutant patients.
PFS and OS curves showed lower confidence‑interval bounds exceeding those of standard second‑line chemotherapy, supporting a survival benefit.
Preclinical models demonstrated durable tumor regression and prevention of relapse when Deruxon Rasib was combined with covalent KRAS G12C inhibitor (Aleron) or G12D inhibitor (Zoldon).
Combination of Deruxon Rasib with gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel improved durability of response in pancreatic cancer models, informing the frontline RESOLUTE‑303 trial design.
Resistance mechanisms identified include amplification of the mutant KRAS allele (≈60% of resistant cases) and non‑genomic adaptations (~40%).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Amplification of mutant KRAS allele observed in ~60% of resistant patient samples.
Non‑genomic resistance mechanisms (e.g., pathway re‑activation) account for the remaining resistant cases.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target both on‑state (covalent) and off‑state (tricomplex) KRAS pools to prevent adaptive resistance.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in resistant KRAS G12C models

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual inhibition of distinct KRAS mutant alleles to block compensatory signaling.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Durable tumor control in KRAS G12D allograft models

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine RAS pathway blockade with cytotoxic chemotherapy to target complementary tumor cell states in pancreatic cancer.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved response durability versus chemotherapy alone in PDAC models

combination_strategies.rationale: Simultaneously trap KRAS in GDP state while Deruxon Rasib promotes GTP hydrolysis, covering both conformations.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Strong synergistic antitumor activity in pan‑KRAS‑amplified resistant models

open_questions: What are the specific non‑genomic mechanisms driving resistance in the 40% of patients without KRAS amplification?
Can cyclophilin A expression levels predict response or resistance to tricomplex inhibitors?

patent_or_ip_hints: US Patent Application for KRAS tricomplex inhibitors (filed 2022)
